3	description: Split ONE high-effort blueprint declaration into a \uses-linked chain of smaller, easier sub-lemmas — each with its own statement, informal proof, and \uses — so the prover formalizes small pieces instead of one large goal. On-demand; iterative (re-dispatch to break a still-hard sub-lemma further, down to sentence level).
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against ONE target declaration that is too big to formalize in
10	    one go — a high-`effort_local` node, or one the progress-critic flags as a
11	    repeated blocker. I am most valuable on a **frontier** node (deps already
12	    done): it is the live bottleneck, and decomposing it exposes small ready
13	    pieces next iter.
14	  - Name the target label in `## Target`, and say **how aggressively to break**
15	    in `## Granularity`: "one level" (the proof's main steps) by default, or
16	    "fine — one mathematical claim per lemma" when a prior, coarser break still
17	    left a hard piece. Decomposition is ITERATIVE and has no floor: if a break
18	    doesn't drop the difficulty enough, re-dispatch me on the still-hard
19	    sub-lemma asking me to break it further (sentence-by-sentence, like the
20	    fine-grained prover). Do not pre-empt this — just break, see if it helped,
21	    break more if not.
22	  - Give me the proof sketch / reference structure to cut along in
23	    `## Proof structure` so I split at real mathematical seams, not arbitrary
24	    points.
25	  - Do NOT dispatch me at an already-done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) — there
26	    is nothing to break. Do NOT dispatch me at an ∞-effort node (no informal
27	    proof to decompose) — send a dag-walker / blueprint-writer to write the
28	    proof first, then me to break it.
29	  - **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** (sync_leanok's job). `\mathlibok` only
30	    on genuine Mathlib anchors.
31	---
32	
33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.
38	
39	## Directive Format
40	
41	```markdown
42	# Effort Breaker Directive
43	
44	## Slug
45	<slug>
46	
47	## Target
48	<the blueprint label to break, e.g. thm:flattening_stratification_exists>
49	
50	## Granularity
51	<"one level" (the proof's main steps) | "fine — one mathematical claim per
52	 lemma" (when a coarser break already happened and a piece is still hard)>
53	
54	## Proof structure
55	<the seams to cut along: the steps of the target's informal proof, or the
56	 structure of the source proof. The dispatcher gives you this so you split at
57	 real mathematical boundaries.>
58	
59	## Strategy context
60	<the slice of STRATEGY.md that matters — what this result supports. You do NOT
61	 read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
62	
63	## References
64	- <path/to/reference.md>: <which proof / steps live here>
65	```
66	
67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.
78	
79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.
80	
81	## Markers and citation (same rules as every blueprint agent)
82	
83	- **Never add `\leanok`** — it is the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase's job.
84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.
95	
96	## Workflow
97	
98	1. Read the directive and the target block on disk.
99	2. Read the references the directive names; identify the proof's steps.
100	3. Author the sub-lemma chain (statement + proof + `\uses` for each), bottom-up so later lemmas can `\uses{}` earlier ones.
101	4. Rewrite the target's proof to invoke the chain.
102	5. For any step needing a source you lack, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
103	6. Re-query the graph; confirm the target's effort dropped and no `\uses{}` broke.
104	7. Write your report.
105	
106	## Logging
107	
108	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/effort-breaker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
109	
110	```markdown
111	# Effort Breaker Report
112	
113	## Slug
114	<slug>
115	
116	## Target
117	<label>
118	
119	## Status
120	<COMPLETE — target re-expressed as a chain | PARTIAL — a step could not be broken/proved (listed below)>
121	
122	## Effort before → after
123	- target `effort_local`: <before> → <after>
124	- sub-lemmas added: <count>
125	
126	## Chain added (target ← L_n ← … ← L1)
127	- `\label{lem:L1}` `\lean{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <statement, one line> (effort ≈ <small>)
128	- `\label{lem:L2}` … — <statement> (\uses{lem:L1})
129	- ...
130	- Target `<label>` proof rewritten: `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}`.
131	
132	## Still hard (re-break candidates)
133	- `lem:Lk` — still large; re-dispatch the breaker at finer granularity, OR <why it's irreducible>.
134	
135	## Could not decompose (strategy items)
136	- <step> — <why: genuinely atomic / novel / statement looks wrong>.
137	
138	## References consulted
139	- `references/<file>.md` — <verbatim content taken>.
140	
141	## Notes for dispatcher
142	- `\lean{}` names I assigned by convention (confirm/scaffold): <list>.
143	- needed macros / cross-chapter concerns.
144	```
145	
146	## Return Value
147	
148	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |

62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |

70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1740	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1741	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1742	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1743	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1744	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1745	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1746	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1747	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1748	\end{lemma}
1749	
1750	\begin{proof}
1751	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1752	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1753	        lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi,
1754	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1755	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1756	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1757	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1758	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1759	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\). Cover extraction
1760	  \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated} gives a finite
1761	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) spanning the unit ideal, with each \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\))
1762	  affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) globally generated by finitely many sections, equivalently a
1763	  finite-free epimorphism \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\). The
1764	  free-epi base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi} applied on each affine \(D(g)\)
1765	  makes \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). The locality reduction
1766	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover} then glues these per-member
1767	  finiteness statements to give \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1768	\end{proof}
1769	
1770	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1771	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1772	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1773	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1774	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1775	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1776	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1777	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1778	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1779	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1780	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1781	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1782	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1783	\end{lemma}
1784	
1785	\begin{proof}
1786	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1787	  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
1788	  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
1789	  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
1790	  base ring.
1791	
1792	  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
1793	  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1794	  \(A_f\).
1795	
1796	  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
1797	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
1798	  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
1799	  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
1800	  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
1801	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
1802	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
1803	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
1804	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
1805	  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
1806	  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
1807	  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
1808	  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
1809	  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
1810	  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
1811	  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).
1812	
1813	  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
1814	  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
1815	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
1816	  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
1817	  source.
1818	
1819	  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
1820	  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
1821	  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
1822	  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
1823	  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
1824	  \(A_f\).
1825	
1826	  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
1827	  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
1828	  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
1829	  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
1830	  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
1831	  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
1832	  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
1833	  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
1834	  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
1835	  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
1836	  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine.
1837	
1838	  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
1839	  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
1840	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1841	\end{proof}
1842	
1843	
1844	\begin{theorem}
1845	
1846	
1847	
1848	\leanok
1849	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1850	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1851	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1852	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1853	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1854	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1855	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1856	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1857	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1858	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1859	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1860	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1861	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1862	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1863	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1864	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1865	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1866	
1867	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1868	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1869	\end{theorem}
1870	
1871	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1872	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1873	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1874	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1875	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1876	% by restricting to a non-empty
1877	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1878	\begin{proof}
1879	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1880	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1881	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1882	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1883	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1884	
1885	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1886	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1887	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1888	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1889	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1890	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1891	
1892	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1893	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1894	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1895	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1896	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1897	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1898	
1899	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1900	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1901	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1902	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1903	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1904	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1905	
1906	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1907	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1908	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1909	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1910	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1911	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1912	\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
  base ring.

  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
  \(A_f\).

  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).

  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
  source.

  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
  \(A_f\).

  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine.

  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat, lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base,
        lem:gf_flat_base_local_on_source, lem:gf_stalk_flat_localBase,
        lem:mathlib_flat_of_localized_maximal,
        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
  base ring. The proof is the synthesis of the four sub-results
  \cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat,lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base,
  lem:gf_flat_base_local_on_source,lem:gf_stalk_flat_localBase}.

  \emph{Pointwise flatness on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Combining the per-patch freeness-to-flatness
  passage \cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat} with the stalk-localization computation
  \cref{lem:gf_stalk_flat_over_base} (which in turn uses
  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), every
  stalk \(\F_x\) with \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,\,p(x)}\).

  \emph{Descent to section flatness.} Fix affine \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), and
  write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a
  \(B\)-module and \(B\) an \(R\)-algebra. By the base-maximal locality criterion
  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_localized_maximal} it suffices to show, for every maximal ideal
  \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over the local ring
  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) corresponds to
  \(\mathfrak p\) (and \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of \(A_f\)). The source-locality
  criterion \cref{lem:gf_flat_base_local_on_source}, applied with base ring \(\OO_{S,x}\),
  source ring \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\) and module \(N_{\mathfrak p}\), reduces this to
  flatness over \(\OO_{S,x}\) of each localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at a maximal ideal
  of the source — equivalently, of each stalk \(\F_y\) for the source points \(y \in W\) with
  \(p(y)\) a generization of \(x\). Each such stalk is flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\) by
  \cref{lem:gf_stalk_flat_localBase}.

  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for every
  maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
\end{proof}
